very specific questions. Which first, he asks, shall I go up into any of the cities of Judah?

he refuses to walk through it without a direct green light.

he maintained really friendly relations with the elders of Judah, specifically around Hebron.

and he brings all 600 of his loyal,

He's a seasoned, battle-hardened general with immense influence over the military.

Abner's strategy is the exact opposite of David's.

Abner just forces a human solution.

It is.

He declares Bishbosheth king over Gilead, Ephraim, Benjamin, and basically all the northern tribes, everything except Judah.

I was reading.
I was reading Robert Alter's analysis of this specific exchange in the art of biblical narrative, and it blew my mind.

used to stick the spear into the ground when they camped.

We go from the adrenaline of a high-speed chase to an absolute dead stop.

The fastest man on the battlefield is suddenly the only one not moving.

Joab and his other brother Avishai arrive at the scene.

Joab and Avishai track Abner to a place called the Hill of Amah near the wilderness of Gibeon.

They form a tight defensive formation on top of the hill.
They are preparing for a final desperate stand in the twilight.

He shouts down to Joab in the fading light.
He asks this famous, incredibly poetic question.
Shall the sword devour forever?
Do you not know that it will be bitterness in the latter end?
How long will it be before you tell the people to stop chasing their brothers?

completely ignoring the fact that he is the one who suggested the deadly game at the pool just a few hours earlier.

did David inadvertently lay the groundwork for the permanent fracture of the nation?
Think about it.

to gain immediate legitimacy, does it guarantee success?
